title: Revenue Isn't the Threshold: When an Australian SME Is Ready for a Fractional CFO
linkedin:
Most business owners I speak with say the same thing about engaging a fractional CFO: they wish they had done it earlier.

The hesitation is almost always the same. "We're not big enough yet." They're waiting for a revenue milestone — five million, ten million — before they think it makes sense.

But the actual triggers for needing CFO-level thinking have nothing to do with size.

Three situations come up consistently when business owners reflect on the moment things changed.

The first: decisions that require a forward model rather than a retrospective report. Whether to hire, renew a lease, price a major contract, or fund growth from cash or credit — these need structured modelling, not intuition. Without it, owners either guess or delay until the deadline decides for them.

The second: revenue growing but the bank account not following. This is the most recognisable signal. The business is busy. Revenue is up. But cash feels tighter than the numbers suggest it should. That gap is a working capital and cash flow problem — and it's a CFO problem, not a bookkeeping problem.

The third: spending hours on financial administration that isn't producing decision-ready output. Pulling numbers for a lender meeting. Building a rough forecast from scratch. These tasks sit above bookkeeping and below what a compliance accountant handles. They quietly consume a week.

July is the cleanest entry point for this kind of support. EOFY data is fresh, FY27 decisions are still open, and the return typically shows in the first quarter — through decisions made faster, costs spotted earlier, and pricing held more deliberately.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#FractionalCFO #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
Here's a question worth sitting with as the new financial year starts.

When was the last time you made a major financial decision — a hire, a pricing change, a capital investment — with a proper forward model behind it? Or did it come down to gut feel and a reasonable guess?

Most business owners are doing the latter. Not because they aren't capable, but because no one has built the tools. That's the gap a fractional CFO fills — and the trigger for needing one has far more to do with the decisions you're making than the revenue you're turning over.

What's the financial decision you're about to make that you'd most like clearer numbers behind?

#FractionalCFO #BusinessOwners
google:
A question we hear regularly from Queensland and NSW business owners at the start of a new financial year: is my business big enough to need a fractional CFO?

The honest answer is that revenue is rarely the right threshold. The more useful question is whether the decisions the business is currently making are getting the financial input they deserve.

Three situations tend to signal the right time. Major decisions — hiring, pricing, capital investment — are being made without a forward model. Revenue is growing but cash is not following, and the owner cannot pinpoint why. Or the owner is spending meaningful hours each week on financial analysis that is not producing output they feel confident acting on.

The start of the new financial year is one of the best moments to address this. EOFY data is fresh, and the decisions for FY27 are still shapeable. Beginning a fractional CFO engagement in July means the year ahead is planned from a complete dataset rather than pieced together mid-year.

ProfitPulse works with owner-led businesses across Brisbane, South East Queensland, Sydney, and Melbourne at exactly this stage. Read the full piece on the blog to understand what the transition looks like in practice.
